comments:
Summary
The paper investigates learning navigation policies end-to-end with deep reinforcement learning (A3C) augmented by auxiliary self-/unsupervised losses: low-resolution depth prediction (either regression or classification) and loop-closure detection. A stacked two-layer LSTM policy network is used and extra proprioceptive inputs (agent-relative velocity, previous action and previous reward) are appended. Experiments on several visually rich 3D maze variants in DeepMind Lab show that (i) adding auxiliary objectives significantly accelerates learning and improves final performance versus standard A3C baselines, (ii) depth classification is superior to depth regression and superior to providing depth as input, (iii) loop-closure detection is learnable and useful, and (iv) analyses (position decoding, t-SNE of LSTM states, latency metrics) indicate the agents learn implicit localization and goal-directed behaviours. A number of ablations and comparisons (e.g. reward-prediction auxiliary task) are reported; experiments use many seeds and hyperparameter sampling and contain useful diagnostic plots and appendices.

Strengths
- The problem is important — learning navigation from pixels in partially observable visually rich 3D environments. The paper focuses on data efficiency, which is a practical bottleneck for RL.
- The proposed use of compact auxiliary tasks (depth prediction from the agent’s own representation and loop-closure classification) is conceptually simple, well-motivated, and appears effective.
- Broad empirical evaluation: multiple maze sizes, static vs random-goal variants, I-maze, many random seeds, hyperparameter sampling, and several ablations (placement of depth loss, classification vs regression, reward-prediction comparison). Analyses (position decoding, F1 for loop closure, t-SNE of LSTM states, latency after goal discovery) strengthen the claims about learned capabilities beyond scalar reward.
- Practical architecture/implementation details are provided (conv layers, LSTM sizes, depth quantization, hyperparameter ranges). The authors investigate the subtle but important issue of reward clipping and show auxiliary losses help stabilize training in some settings.

Weaknesses and concerns
- Use of privileged information at training time: the depth target and loop-closure labels are derived from simulator internals (Z-buffer and integrated odometry/position thresholds). While this is reasonable for simulated research, the reliance on simulator-provided depth and precise velocity/position signals limits claims about applicability to real-world agents unless the paper discusses how these signals could be obtained in practice (e.g. stereo/active sensing or SLAM). The manuscript should more explicitly acknowledge this limitation and discuss robustness to noisy or absent privileged signals.
- Comparability / reward-clipping inconsistency: the baselines are reported with reward clipping since plain A3C becomes unstable without it, while some auxiliary agents are shown without clipping (or marked with asterisk). This complicates direct apples-to-apples comparisons. The paper discusses this, but more consistent controlled experiments (e.g. same clipping policy for all agents, or explicit normalization of results) would make conclusions stronger.
- Causality of improvement: auxiliary tasks add extra training signal and possibly more gradient updates per frame. The authors attempt to address this (Table 3, doubling baseline updates) and argue the benefit is not only faster learning. Still, a clearer controlled study matching total gradient updates and wall-clock compute between models would better isolate representation benefits from extra optimization steps.
- Loop-closure labeling specifics: thresholds η1, η2 and the method (integrating 2D velocity) are described, but sensitivity to these thresholds and to odometry noise is not explored. Since loop-closure is proposed as an important auxiliary signal, more analysis of robustness and necessity would help.
- Limited comparison to other recent approaches: while the related work cites several methods, the empirical comparisons are primarily to A3C variants and an implementation of reward-prediction. It would be valuable to compare against more recent memory/external-memory architectures or models that incorporate explicit mapping (even if approximate), or at least discuss how those alternatives may fare.
- Some clarity/repetition issues in the text and Appendix (duplicated paragraphs) make it harder to follow in places. A focused cleanup would improve presentation.

Suggestions for improvement / additional experiments
- Report experiments where depth and/or loop-closure targets are corrupted or noisy to test robustness (simulate realistic sensors or noisy odometry). Also examine training when depth is not available at all (i.e. only loop-closure) to quantify the individual contribution in more realistic settings.
- Provide a controlled experiment equating number of gradient updates and/or total compute between baseline and auxiliary models to better argue that auxiliary objectives improve representation and not merely optimization throughput.
- Explore generalization: test agents trained on fixed maze geometry but novel textures/lighting, or on procedurally generated mazes to judge if learned representations generalize beyond memorized layouts.
- Discuss path toward transferring the approach to real robots: how to obtain depth/loop-closure signals, or how to replace them with sensors/self-supervision derived from onboard modalities.
- Consider including or discussing external-memory baselines (NTM, DNC, attention-based memory) for tasks requiring more sustained memory to evaluate whether stacked LSTMs are the limiting factor.

Minor points
- Provide clearer justification for feeding reward and visual features to different LSTM layers (currently mostly a post-hoc hypothesis). Ablation where these inputs are shuffled to layers would be informative.
- Clean up duplicated text in Appendix and ensure all hyperparameters and thresholds are easy to find.

Overall assessment
This paper presents a solid empirical study showing that simple, online auxiliary tasks can substantially improve data-efficiency and performance for end-to-end learned navigation in challenging 3D environments. The experiments are extensive and several useful analyses are provided. The main limitations are the use of simulator-provided privileged signals (depth and accurate velocity) and some remaining uncertainty about whether the observed benefits stem purely from additional gradient signal versus improved representations. With clarifications, robustness analyses, and a cleanup of presentation, this work would make a substantial contribution to learning-based navigation research. I therefore recommend acceptance (strongly positive score).
